title: 4903: time
content:
A creation of God which marks the duration of life and which is measured by changes in the created order. The flow of time is directed by God who appoints particular “times” within his unfolding purposes. Because human life is brief, time should be used properly, making the most of every opportunity.

Time as a creation of God

Time began at creation

GEN 1:5 See also 1CO 2:7

Time is marked by change in the created order

GEN 1:14 The main divisions of time were based upon the phases of the sun and moon. See also PSA 104:19; JER 33:20

The timelessness of God

PSA 90:2 See also GEN 1:1; PSA 93:2; PRO 8:22–8:26; HAB 1:12; JHN 1:1–1:2

God's perception of time

2PE 3:8 See also PSA 90:4

Measurement of time

Divisions of time

GEN 1:8 A day was marked by the regular cycle of evening and morning; ; NUM 28:14 Months were determined by the regular phases of the moon; ; 1KI 4:7 The year consisted of twelve lunar months; ; JHN 11:9 An hour was one twelfth of the period of daylight.

Indefinite periods of time

1SA 1:22; ; JER 32:14; ; LUK 20:9; ; ACT 14:28, ACT 18:23

Recurring times and seasons

God's seasonal provision

DEU 11:14 See also LEV 26:4; DEU 28:12; PSA 145:15–145:16; JER 5:24; ACT 14:17

Appointed times for festivals

LEV 23:4 See also EXO 13:10, EXO 23:15 parallel EXO 34:18; NUM 9:2–9:3

A right time for every activity

ECC 3:1–3:8 See also ECC 3:11, ECC 8:6; JER 8:7

Times of human life in God's hands

PSA 31:15, PSA 139:16 See also JOB 14:5; DAN 2:21; ACT 17:26

Appointed times within God's unfolding purpose

The time of fulfilment of promise

The time of Isaac's birth: GEN 21:2; ; ROM 9:9; LUK 1:20 the time of John the Baptist's birth; ; ACT 7:17 the time of Israel's possession of the land

The time of Israel's deliverance

PSA 102:13 See also HAB 2:3, HAB 3:16

The time of Jesus Christ's coming

GAL 4:2–4:4 See also MRK 1:15; LUK 19:44; 1TI 2:6; TIT 1:3

The time of Jesus Christ's death

JHN 12:23 See also MAT 26:18; JHN 2:4, JHN 8:20, JHN 13:1; ROM 5:6; 1PE 1:11

The time of Jesus Christ's return

1CO 4:5 See also MAT 24:36; EPH 1:10; HEB 9:28; 1TI 6:15

The time of God's judgment

PSA 75:2; ; EZK 7:7–7:8, EZK 24:14; ; ZEP 2:2; ; MAT 8:29; ; REV 11:18

The appointed time of the end

DAN 8:19, DAN 11:27, DAN 11:29; ; MRK 13:32; ; ACT 1:6–1:7

The time between Jesus Christ's first and second coming

The time of salvation

2CO 6:2 The time of salvation lies between the two comings of Jesus Christ. See also ISA 49:8; HEB 11:13

The end times

ACT 2:17; ; HEB 1:2; ; 1PE 1:20; ; 1JN 2:18

Proper use of time

Recognising the brevity of human life

PSA 90:12 See also PSA 39:4–39:6; PRO 27:1; JAS 4:14

Seeking God

ISA 55:6 See also PSA 32:6, PSA 69:13, PSA 95:7–95:8; ECC 12:1, ECC 12:6–12:7

Looking to eternal realities

1CO 7:29–7:31 See also PSA 10:6; ISA 56:12; LUK 12:16–12:21; ROM 13:11–13:12; JAS 5:7–5:9

Making the most of opportunities

COL 4:5 See also GAL 6:10; EPH 5:16

title: 4906: abolition
content:
The final ending and elimination of practices or states of affairs. Scripture looks forward to God's final abolition of the present order, through the inauguration of the new Jerusalem.

Things to be abolished in the kingdom of God

War and destruction

HOS 2:18 See also PSA 46:9–46:10; ISA 9:5; ZEC 9:10

Illness and suffering

EXO 23:25–23:26 See also EXO 15:26; DEU 7:15; ISA 53:4; MAT 8:16–8:17 parallels MRK 1:32–1:34, LUK 4:40–4:41

Sin

ROM 6:6 See also EZK 36:25–36:26 God speaks here of his abolition of sinful, human hard-heartedness; ROM 8:3–8:4; HEB 9:26

Death and mourning

1CO 15:24–15:26; REV 21:1–21:4 See also ISA 25:7–25:8, ISA 65:17–65:20; 1CO 15:54–15:55; 2TI 1:10; REV 20:13–20:14

The abolition of idolatry in Israel

Israel was commanded to abolish Canaanite worship

EXO 23:24 See also EXO 34:13; NUM 33:50–33:52; DEU 12:2–12:3

The abolition of mediums and spiritists

DEU 18:9–18:12; ; 1SA 28:3–28:9; ; 2KI 23:24

In Asa's reign

1KI 15:11–15:13 parallel 2CH 14:2–14:3

In Hezekiah's reign

2KI 18:4 parallel 2CH 31:1

In Josiah's reign

2KI 23:4–23:20 parallels 2CH 34:3–34:7, 2CH 34:33

The abolition of the temple

Sacrifice in the temple abolished

DAN 11:31 Antiochus Epiphanes abolished the daily sacrifice in the temple in 168 B.C., thereby attacking the symbolic centre of Israel's life. See also DAN 12:11

Jesus Christ was accused of seeking to abolish the temple

MAT 26:61 parallel MRK 14:58 See also MAT 21:12–21:13 parallels MRK 11:15–11:17, LUK 19:45–19:46 Jesus Christ's action may have been understood as a symbolic abolition of a defiled sacrificial system; MAT 27:39–27:40 parallel MRK 15:29–15:30; ACT 6:14

The final abolition of the temple in the new Jerusalem

REV 21:22

Abolition and the law

The law has not been abolished

MAT 5:17–5:19 See also ROM 3:31

The law has been abolished as an instrument of condemnation

COL 2:13–2:14 See also 2CO 3:6–3:17; EPH 2:14–2:16; HEB 10:1–10:10

The law does not abolish God's promises

GAL 3:17–3:18 God did not abolish his promise of righteousness to Abraham when he gave the law, because the promise was not conditional upon keeping the law but is received by faith. See also ROM 4:14

The offence of the cross is abolished by accepting circumcision

GAL 5:11 Jews found offensive the Christians' claim that salvation did not come through keeping the law, but instead through faith in a crucified Messiah, whom the law had cursed. Paul regarded “the offence of the cross” as central to the gospel, and refused to make salvation conditional upon circumcision and obedience to the law. See also 1CO 1:22–1:24, 1CO 2:2; GAL 3:13, GAL 5:2, GAL 6:14–6:15

title: 4909: beginning
content:
A starting point, origin or source. The beginning often refers to the time of or before the creation of the world. God is described as the Beginning and the End, denoting the eternity of his existence. Jesus Christ's coming marks a new beginning both for God's people and, finally, for all creation.

Beginning as the starting point

As the source

PSA 111:10 See also PRO 1:7, PRO 9:10; MIC 1:13 the source of Israel's sin; COL 1:18 Jesus Christ as the source of the church's life

As the origin

JOB 8:7; ; ACT 26:4; ; HEB 7:3

As the commencement

LEV 23:5; ; JDG 7:19; ; 1KI 6:1 parallel 2CH 3:1; ; LUK 23:54; ; 1JN 2:7; ; 2JN 1:5

The beginning: before the creation

PRO 8:22–8:23

Jesus Christ's existence predates the creation

JHN 17:5 See also JHN 1:1–1:2, JHN 8:58; COL 1:15–1:17; 1JN 1:1, 1JN 2:13–2:14

Salvation planned before the creation

EPH 1:3–1:4 See also 1CO 2:7; 2TH 2:13; 2TI 1:9; TIT 1:2

The beginning: the creation

GEN 1:1; HEB 1:10 See also PSA 102:25; ISA 40:21; DAN 12:1; MAT 24:21 parallel MRK 13:19

Marriage ordained from the beginning

MAT 19:4–19:6 parallel MRK 10:6–10:9 See also GEN 1:27, GEN 2:24

The beginning of Israel's nationhood

EXO 12:2 See also 2SA 7:10 parallel 1CH 17:9; ISA 52:4; JER 2:2–2:3

New beginnings for God's people

For Israel

HOS 2:14–2:15 See also ISA 1:26, ISA 43:18–43:19, ISA 44:26, ISA 61:4; JER 33:7–33:9

For believers

2CO 5:17 See also 2CO 4:6; EPH 2:10

A new creation

ISA 65:17 See also ISA 66:22; 2PE 3:13; REV 21:1

Beginnings of significant events

The rebuilding of Jerusalem

NEH 2:18; ; HAG 1:14

Jesus Christ's ministry

MAT 4:17 See also MRK 1:1, MRK 1:15; LUK 3:23; ACT 1:1

The beginning of signs of the end

MAT 24:7–24:8 parallel MRK 13:8; ; LUK 21:28

The beginning and the end

Implying all-inclusiveness

GEN 44:12; ; DEU 31:24; ; 1SA 3:12; ; MAT 20:8

The beginning and end contrasted

PRO 20:21; ; ECC 7:8; ; 2PE 2:20

Completing what is begun

PHP 1:6 See also 2CO 8:6; GAL 3:3; HEB 3:14; REV 2:5, REV 2:19

God as the Beginning and the End

ISA 44:6; REV 21:6 See also ISA 41:4, ISA 48:12; REV 1:17, REV 2:8, REV 22:13

Satan as sinful from the beginning

JHN 8:44 See also 1JN 3:8

title: 4912: chance
content:
The occurrence of events at random, as undesigned happenings. While people may perceive events as random, in Scripture all events are subject to God's sovereignty.

Chance in human life

Chance events

2CH 18:33–18:34 parallel 1KI 22:34–22:38 See also 1SA 6:7–6:9; PRO 26:10

Human life seems to be governed by chance, not merit

ECC 9:11 See also ECC 2:17–2:19

Providence at work through apparent chance

JON 1:17 See also RUT 2:3; LUK 23:44–23:45 parallels MAT 27:45, MAT 27:51, MRK 15:33, MRK 15:38; ACT 16:26

Chance events overruled by God's sovereignty

The casting of lots and the element of chance

PRO 16:33 See also NUM 33:53–33:54; JDG 20:8–20:10; NEH 10:34, NEH 11:1; LUK 23:34 parallels MAT 27:35, MRK 15:24; ACT 1:23–1:26

Urim and Thummim, seeming to be dependent on chance

EXO 28:29–28:30 The Urim and Thummim were used by the high priest to declare God's will. It was a form of casting lots showing either an affirmative or negative response to an enquiry. See also NUM 27:21; 1SA 28:4–28:6; EZR 2:62–2:63 parallel NEH 7:64–7:65

title: 4915: completion
content:
The bringing to fulfilment of a work, such as the work of creation or redemption.

Creation was completed by the seventh day

GEN 2:1–2:2; DEU 32:4 See also GEN 1:3–1:4 Genesis chapter 1 records the creation completed on each successive day as being “good”; EXO 20:11; JHN 1:3; COL 1:16; HEB 1:2, HEB 4:3, HEB 11:3

All things are completed in God's time

GAL 4:4 See also GEN 15:16; DAN 9:24; MRK 1:15; ROM 5:6; EPH 1:10

The work of redemption is a completed work

Jesus Christ brought his earthly work to completion

JHN 17:4, JHN 19:30 See also JHN 4:34, JHN 5:36

Jesus Christ will present his church complete to his Father

JHN 6:37–6:40, JHN 6:45, JHN 17:24

Jesus Christ makes his people complete in himself

EPH 3:19; PHP 1:6; COL 2:10 See also JHN 15:11, JHN 16:24; ROM 15:14; COL 2:2; JAS 1:4

Believers are to complete their God- given work

ACT 20:24 See also ACT 14:26; ROM 15:28; COL 4:17; 2TI 4:7

The completion of the age

This world will come to an end

ISA 65:17 See also ISA 66:22; MAT 19:28; EPH 1:9–1:10; HEB 1:11–1:12; 2PE 3:10–3:12; REV 21:1–21:5

There will be a final separation of the righteous and wicked

MAT 13:36–13:43, MAT 13:47–13:50, MAT 25:32–25:41; ; 2TH 1:8–1:10

title: 4918: dawn
content:
The time marked by the rising of the sun. It signals the end of night and relief from its attendant concerns, and the approach of daylight with the opportunity for renewed daily activity. The light of dawn following darkness is a symbol of hope and vindication, fulfilled especially in the coming of the Messiah through whom God's new age dawns.

Dawn as the end of the night

It ends the darkness of the night

AMO 5:8 See also 2SA 23:4; JOB 41:18

It signals the end of nightly activity

GEN 32:22–32:26; ; JDG 19:25–19:26; ; 1SA 14:36, 1SA 25:36; ; 2SA 2:32, 2SA 17:22; ; SNG 2:16–2:17

Waiting for the dawn to bring relief

PSA 130:6 See also JOB 7:3–7:4; PSA 46:5; ISA 21:12, ISA 38:13; ACT 27:29

Dawn as the beginning of a new day

It provides the earliest opportunity of the day

DAN 6:19 First light provides the earliest opportunity for action and implies urgency. See also GEN 19:15; JOS 6:15 Dawn was a common time for military campaigns to begin; JDG 9:33, JDG 16:2; 1SA 19:11; MAT 28:1 parallels MRK 16:2, LUK 24:1 Women visit Jesus Christ's tomb at dawn; MRK 1:35 parallel LUK 4:42 Jesus Christ prays at dawn; LUK 22:66; JHN 8:2; ACT 27:33

Important activity begins at dawn

NEH 4:21 See also NEH 8:3

Dawn as a symbol of blessings to come

The Messiah brings a new dawn

ISA 9:2; REV 22:16 See also NUM 24:17; MAT 4:16; LUK 1:78–1:79; JHN 1:4–1:5, JHN 8:12

A symbol of hope and vindication

PSA 37:6, PSA 112:4 See also JDG 5:31; PSA 57:8 parallel PSA 108:2; PRO 4:18; ISA 58:8, ISA 60:1–60:3, ISA 62:1

A symbol of God's coming in glory

HAB 3:3–3:4 See also MAL 4:2

A symbol of renewal

PSA 110:3

Dawn as a symbol of God's revelation

1JN 2:8 See also ISA 8:20; 2PE 1:19

Dawn as a time of reckoning

Darkness at dawn as a picture of judgment

ISA 13:10–13:11 See also JOB 3:9; AMO 4:13

The light of dawn exposes the wicked

JOB 38:12–38:13; ; JHN 3:19–3:20; ; EPH 5:11–5:13

title: 4921: day
content:
A period of 24 hours or, as distinct from night, the period of daylight. The term may also be used more generally to refer to a point in time. Daylight is used figuratively to describe the illumination afforded to believers, who are urged to use the opportunities it presents and to avoid the evil usually associated with darkness.

Day and daylight

Day as a period of 24 hours

GEN 1:8; ; MAT 20:19; ; JHN 4:43, JHN 11:6

Day as the period of daylight

GEN 1:5 See also GEN 1:14–1:18, GEN 8:22; PSA 74:16; JER 33:20

Daylight gives opportunity for activity

A time for human endeavour

JHN 11:9–11:10 See also JOS 10:12–10:13; ACT 27:29

A time of spiritual opportunity

JHN 9:4–9:5 See also JHN 12:35–12:36

Daylight allows things to be seen

Daylight activities are seen by all

2SA 12:11–12:12; ; EZK 12:3–12:4; ; MAT 10:27 parallel LUK 12:3

Examples of those avoiding daylight

JDG 6:27 Gideon; ; JHN 3:2 Nicodemus

Daylight activities should be decent

ROM 13:12–13:13 See also EPH 5:8–5:14; 1TH 5:5–5:8; 2PE 2:13

Six working days ordained

EXO 20:8–20:11 parallel DEU 5:12–5:15

Particular days of the week

The Sabbath (Saturday)

EXO 20:8–20:10 parallel DEU 5:12–5:15 The Sabbath was reckoned from sunset on Friday to sunset on Saturday. See also LUK 4:16; COL 2:16; HEB 4:4

The Day of Preparation for the Sabbath (Friday)

MRK 15:42 parallel LUK 23:54; ; JHN 19:31

The first day of the week (Sunday)

ACT 20:7 See also MRK 16:2 parallels LUK 24:1, JHN 20:1; MRK 16:9; 1CO 16:2; REV 1:10

Festival days

LEV 23:5–23:6 Passover and Unleavened Bread; ; LEV 23:27–23:28 the Day of Atonement; LEV 23:34–23:36; ; JHN 7:37; NUM 28:26 the day of firstfruits

Day as a point in time

The day of salvation

2CO 6:2 See also ISA 25:9, ISA 49:8

God sends the day of disaster

DEU 32:35; ; JER 17:18, JER 51:2; ; MRK 13:19

God helps on the day of distress

PSA 86:7 See also GEN 35:3; 2SA 22:19 parallel PSA 18:18; 2KI 19:3–19:4 parallel ISA 37:3–37:4; PSA 94:13; JER 17:17

The final days of time

The last days

ACT 2:17–2:18 See also JOL 2:28–2:29; ISA 2:2 parallel MIC 4:1; HOS 3:5; 1TI 4:1; 2TI 3:1; HEB 1:2; 1PE 1:20

The day of the LORD

ISA 13:9–13:11 JER 30:7–30:9; ; ZEP 3:16–3:17; ; PHP 1:6; ; 2TI 1:12; JOL 2:1–2:2; ; OBA 1:15; ; ZEP 1:14–1:15; 1TH 5:2; ; 2PE 3:10

The last day

JHN 6:39–6:40 See also JHN 11:24, JHN 12:48

References to specific periods of days at the end of history

DAN 12:11–12:12; ; REV 11:3, REV 12:6

God's different perception of time

2PE 3:8 See also PSA 90:4

title: 4924: delay
content: A postponement or pause, which can result from a deliberate decision or a failure to achieve the intended objective in the time available. Scripture stresses that apparent delays on the part of God are due to his love and wisdom, especially to give sinners time for repentance.

title: 4925: delay, divine
content:
Delays by God do not result from any failure on his part, but reflect unrealistic human expectations concerning his actions. God delays his vengeance in order to give time for human repentance.

God delays to allow for repentance

To give time for repentance

LUK 13:6–13:9; ROM 2:4; 2PE 3:8–3:9 See also MAT 3:7–3:11 parallel LUK 3:7–3:10; 1TI 2:4; JAS 5:7; REV 2:21

In response to repentance

1KI 21:29 See also 1KI 21:21–21:28

Where there is no repentance, God does not delay

EZK 12:25 See also EZK 21:24–21:26; REV 2:16

God delays judgment for the sake of his name

ISA 48:9 See also NEH 9:30–9:31

Jesus Christ delayed visiting Lazarus for God's glory

JHN 11:1–11:6 See also JHN 11:11–11:23, JHN 11:38–11:44

The giving of the Holy Spirit was delayed until Pentecost

LUK 24:49 See also ACT 1:4, ACT 2:1–2:4

God acts when he wants and nothing can delay him

HEB 10:37 Referring to the second coming of Jesus Christ. See also HAB 2:3 Referring to the LORD's delay in sending the Babylonians to conquer Judah and take her inhabitants into exile; REV 22:20

Other examples of delay

EXO 32:1

title: 4926: delay, human
content:
Human delay is often the result of fear, indecision or disobedience to God. Scripture indicates that delay is to be avoided.

Examples of delay as a result of fear

Israel entering the promised land

NUM 13:31–13:32 See also NUM 13:1–13:3, NUM 13:17–13:20, NUM 13:27–13:30, NUM 14:33–14:35

Gideon responding to God's call

JDG 6:36–6:40

Examples of delay as a result of disobedience

Lot escaping from Sodom

GEN 19:16 See also GEN 19:12–19:15

Jonah going to Nineveh

JON 1:3 See also PSA 119:60; JON 1:1–1:2, JON 3:1–3:3 Jonah obeyed the second time.

Nothing should delay believers from following Jesus Christ

MAT 8:21–8:22 parallel LUK 9:59–9:60 See also MAT 9:9; MRK 1:20; LUK 14:15–14:24, LUK 19:5–19:6

People should not delay in the matter of salvation

ISA 55:6 See also AMO 6:3; ACT 24:24–24:26; 2CO 6:2; HEB 2:3

Believers are not to delay in fulfilling their vows to God and obligations to others

ECC 5:4 See also LEV 19:13; DEU 23:21; PSA 61:8

Examples of human responses to delay

Sarah's wrong response brought trouble

GEN 16:1–16:6

Saul's wrong response cost him the kingship

1SA 13:11–13:12 See also 1SA 13:8–13:10, 1SA 13:13–13:14

Jacob responded with patience

GEN 29:20–29:30

Paul responded by giving instructions to Timothy

1TI 3:14–3:15

Daniel responded by praying

DAN 9:19 See also DAN 9:1–9:23, DAN 10:10–10:13 Gabriel explains the reason for the delay.

David reponded by calling on the LORD

PSA 13:1, PSA 40:17, PSA 141:1

title: 4930: end
content:
A limit in time or space, or the outcome of a sequence of actions or events. God knows the end from the beginning. He is without limit: he exercises control over the furthest reaches of the world, and will bring to an end earthly powers, human life and the present age. Those who persevere to the end will be saved.

God is without limit

HEB 1:12 See also PSA 102:26–102:27; DAN 6:26, DAN 7:14; MAT 24:35 parallels MRK 13:31, LUK 21:33; LUK 1:33

God is the Beginning and the End

REV 21:6 See also ISA 44:6; REV 22:13

God brings earthly powers to an end

ISA 13:11; DAN 2:44 See also PSA 89:44; ISA 21:16, ISA 23:9; JER 48:2 Moab; JER 49:37 Elam; EZK 7:2–7:6 Israel; EZK 26:21 Tyre; EZK 30:18 Egypt; DAN 5:26 Babylon

Persevering to the end

MAT 10:22, MAT 24:13; ; PHP 1:6; ; HEB 3:14, HEB 10:36

title: 4933: evening
content:
The time between the fading of daylight and nightfall. The onset of darkness marks the end of the working day and is a time for rest and refreshment. It is an assigned time for sacrifice and an opportunity for reflection and prayer. The evening also brings dangers.

Evening marks the end of the day

Evening and God's work of creation

GEN 1:5

The onset of darkness

PRO 7:9 PSA 102:11, PSA 109:23; JER 6:4

Days reckoned from evening to evening

EXO 12:18; ; LEV 23:32 The Sabbath began at sunset on Friday.

Ritual uncleanness lasts until evening

LEV 11:39–11:40, LEV 17:15; ; NUM 19:7–19:8; ; DEU 23:11

Waiting on God until evening

JOS 7:6 See also JDG 20:23, JDG 20:26–20:27, JDG 21:2–21:3

Mourning until evening

2SA 1:12

City gates shut at evening

JOS 2:5; ; 2KI 7:9–7:11; ; NEH 13:19; ; EZK 46:2

Executed criminals to be buried by evening

DEU 21:22–21:23; ; JOS 8:29, JOS 10:26; ; MAT 27:57–27:58 parallels MRK 15:42–15:43, LUK 23:52–23:54; ; JHN 19:31

Evening as a time for refreshment

A time to rest from labour

PSA 104:23; LUK 21:37 See also JDG 19:16; RUT 2:17; JOB 7:2; ZEP 2:7; MAT 20:8; MRK 4:35–4:38 parallels MAT 8:23, LUK 8:22–8:23

A time to rest from travel

JDG 19:9 See also GEN 19:1–19:2; NUM 9:21; LUK 24:29

A time to return home

MAT 21:17 See also GEN 30:16; MRK 11:11, MRK 11:19; JHN 6:16–6:17

A time to eat

EXO 16:12; ; 1KI 17:6; ; MAT 14:15; ; JHN 13:2

A time for reflection and prayer

GEN 24:63 See also PSA 55:17, PSA 141:2; MAT 14:23 parallel MRK 6:46

Evening religious duties

The evening sacrifice

EXO 29:38–29:41 See also NUM 28:8; 1CH 16:40; EZR 3:31KI 18:29; EZR 9:4–9:5; DAN 9:21

Lighting the lamps in the sanctuary

EXO 30:8 See also EXO 27:21 parallel LEV 24:3

The Passover sacrifice

EXO 12:6 See also LEV 23:5; NUM 9:2–9:5; DEU 16:6; JOS 5:10; MAT 26:20 parallel MRK 14:17–14:18; LUK 22:14

Adulterous behaviour in the evening

2SA 11:2–11:4; ; JOB 24:15; ; PRO 7:14–7:20

Other evening activities

MAT 8:16 parallels MRK 1:32, LUK 4:40 People came to Jesus Christ after sunset, when the Sabbath was over. See also GEN 24:11 Water collected in the evening, the coolest time of the day; MAT 16:2 Red sky in the evening as a sign of fair weather

The dangers of the evening

ISA 17:14; ZEP 3:3 Israel's rulers are likened to wolves who prowl in the evening looking for food. See also 2KI 7:7; PSA 59:6; HAB 1:8

title: 4936: fate
content: A hidden power which some believe to control and determine everything that happens in such a way that it is impossible to prevent or change things. Scripture stresses both the sovereignty of God and human responsibility and has no place for this pagan notion. The term is also used in a looser sense, to mean “end” or “destiny”.

title: 4937: fate, and fatalism
content:
The belief that all things are predetermined, especially by the stars, removing human freedom and responsibility. Scripture affirms human freedom and responsibility and declares that the sun, moon and stars are all part of God's creation and are subject to him.

The heavenly bodies subject to God's authority

The heavenly bodies are created by God

GEN 1:14–1:18; ; JOB 9:9; ; PSA 8:3, PSA 74:16, PSA 148:3–148:5; ; AMO 5:8

The heavenly bodies are subject to the rule and providence of God

ISA 40:26 See also JOB 38:31–38:32; PSA 104:19; JER 31:35; MAT 5:45

The biblical rejection of fatalism in human choice

Human beings are free to choose between right and wrong

DEU 11:26–11:28 See also DEU 30:15–30:19; EZK 18:21–18:23

Human beings are free to call upon the name of the Lord

JOL 2:32; ; ACT 2:21; ; ROM 10:11–10:13

Human beings are free to seek the Lord

1CH 28:9; ; ISA 55:6

Human beings are free to come to the Lord

REV 22:17 See also MAT 11:28

title: 4938: fate, and final destiny
content:
The final destiny or predetermined end of individuals, groups or nations.

Death, the fate of humanity

ECC 7:2 See also ECC 2:14–2:16, ECC 3:19–3:22, ECC 9:2–9:6; HEB 9:27

The fate of the ungodly

ISA 65:11–65:12 See also JOB 8:11–8:19; PSA 49:13–49:20, PSA 73:17; EPH 5:5–5:6; 2PE 2:4–2:9

The fate of the godly

The fate of the godly in terms of God's rewards

2PE 1:10–1:11 See also MAT 5:3–5:10, MAT 25:34; LUK 6:20–6:22; 1CO 2:7; JAS 1:12, JAS 2:5

The fate of the godly in terms of present suffering

2TI 3:12 See also JHN 15:20; 1TH 3:1–3:4; 1PE 4:12–4:14

The fate of individuals

LUK 2:34–2:35 See also 1KI 20:37–20:42, 1KI 21:17–21:26; LUK 22:22 parallels MAT 26:24, MRK 14:21

The fate of human regulations, institutions and earthly realms

DAN 7:27 See also JOB 12:23–12:25; ISA 34:1–34:4; DAN 2:21; MAT 24:35 parallels MRK 13:31, LUK 21:33; COL 2:20–2:22; REV 18:1–18:3

title: 4942: fulness
content:
The totality of something, as it is brought to completion by being filled. Jesus Christ is seen as expressing the full nature and purposes of God, so that believers may rest assured that, through Christ, they know God as he really is. Believers are also being brought to fulness through the gospel, although the final fulness of joy and understanding will take place only in the future kingdom of God.

Jesus Christ, the fulness of God

Jesus Christ, the fulness of God's nature

COL 2:9–2:10 See also COL 1:19

Jesus Christ, the fulness of God's attributes

JHN 1:14 See also JHN 1:16

Christians and the church enjoy fulness through Jesus Christ

EPH 4:11–4:13 See also JHN 10:10; EPH 1:22–1:23, EPH 3:14–3:19; COL 2:9–2:10

Believers receive fulness of joy from God

1PE 1:8–1:9 See also PSA 16:11; JHN 17:13; ACT 16:29–16:34; ROM 15:13

The fulness of knowledge and understanding

Fulness of understanding comes through Jesus Christ

COL 2:2–2:3 See also ROM 15:14; COL 1:9; PHM 1:6

Fulness of understanding is only fully realised when believers see God face to face

1CO 13:12

Believers should be constantly filled with the Holy Spirit

EPH 5:18 See also ACT 2:1–2:4, ACT 4:31, ACT 6:3–6:4, ACT 13:52

Believers receive the fulness of God's love through Jesus Christ

1JN 4:12 See also 1JN 4:16–4:18

God works out his purposes in the fulness of time

EPH 1:9–1:10 See also LUK 21:24 Whatever the precise meaning of the phrase “the times of the Gentiles”, it clearly indicates the sovereignty of God over times and events; GAL 4:1–4:5

Fulness indicating the completion of God's saving activity amongst both Jews and Gentiles

ROM 11:25 See also ROM 11:11–11:12

title: 4945: history
content:
In Scripture, more than a record of and reflection on past events, but a process under God's control which has as its goal the eventual fulfilment of his purposes and through which he makes himself known. The goal of history is realised in Jesus Christ, through whom the age of salvation is inaugurated. Both the OT and NT point to the importance of faith rooted in historical events.

God is active in history

God's control over historical events

DAN 2:21; ACT 17:26 See also GEN 45:5–45:8; 1KI 12:15 parallel 2CH 10:15; DAN 4:25, DAN 7:25; AMO 9:7

God's saving purposes worked out through history

EPH 1:11 See also ISA 14:24–14:27, ISA 37:26, ISA 41:4, ISA 46:10–46:11; ACT 1:7, ACT 2:23

God is known through his people's history

God is known to his people

ISA 46:9 See also EXO 10:2; DEU 4:32–4:35; PSA 77:11–77:15; ISA 43:10–43:13, ISA 44:6–44:8; MIC 6:5

God is revealed to the nations

PSA 126:2 See also 1KI 8:56–8:60

The Israelites were called to remember their history

The past was recalled in festivals

LEV 23:42–23:43; ; DEU 16:1–16:6

The past was recounted to successive generations

PSA 78:2–78:7 See also DEU 32:7–32:9; JDG 6:13; PSA 44:1–44:3

Jesus Christ as the centre of the history of salvation

He is its beginning and end

REV 22:13 See also REV 1:8, REV 1:17, REV 2:8

God's saving purposes in history fulfilled in Jesus Christ

EPH 1:9–1:10 See also ROM 5:6; GAL 4:4; COL 1:17

Jesus Christ inaugurates God's new age

COL 1:18 See also DAN 7:13–7:14; MRK 1:15; MAT 12:28 parallel LUK 11:20; EPH 2:7

The Christian faith is based on the historical facts of salvation

Testimony passed on by eye-witnesses

LUK 1:1–1:4 See also HEB 2:3; 1PE 5:1; 2PE 1:16; 1JN 1:1–1:3

Remembering Jesus Christ's death

1CO 11:23–11:25 parallel LUK 22:19–22:20

Jesus Christ's resurrection as historical fact

ACT 1:3 See also LUK 24:1–24:3, LUK 24:39; JHN 20:27; 1CO 15:3–15:8

Historical references in the NT

LUK 1:1–1:2; ; MAT 2:1; ; LUK 3:1–3:2; ; MAT 14:3–14:4 parallel MRK 6:17–6:18; ; ACT 5:37

Landmarks in salvation history

The creation

GEN 1:1, GEN 2:7

The fall

GEN 3:6–3:7; ; ROM 5:12

The call of Abraham

GEN 12:1–12:3; ; HEB 11:8

The exodus from Egypt

EXO 13:3, EXO 19:4–19:6

The birth of Jesus Christ

JHN 1:14; ; HEB 1:1–1:2

Jesus Christ's death and resurrection

1CO 15:3–15:4; ; PHP 2:6–2:11; ; HEB 1:3–1:4

title: 4948: hour
content:
One twelfth of the period of daylight between sunrise and sunset. Measurement of time in biblical days was often approximate, referring most frequently to the broad divisions of time marked by the third, sixth and ninth hours. The term also denotes a significant or appointed moment or period of time, especially the hour of Jesus Christ's suffering and his return.

Hour as a period of time

A measured interval

JHN 11:9 See also MAT 20:9–20:12 The working day lasted twelve hours; LUK 22:59; ACT 19:34; REV 8:1

Symbolising a brief time

MAT 6:27 parallel LUK 12:25 See also MAT 26:40 parallel MRK 14:37REV 17:12, REV 18:10, REV 18:17, REV 18:19

Symbolising a significant period

ECC 9:12 See also LUK 22:53 Darkness provides an hour of opportunity for the powers of evil.

Hours denoting specific times of day

MAT 20:3–20:5 The third, sixth and ninth hours marked the principal divisions of the day.

The third hour: 9 a.m

MRK 15:25 See also ACT 2:15

The sixth hour: noon

JHN 4:6 See also GEN 43:16; 1KI 18:27; JOB 5:14; MAT 27:45 parallels MRK 15:33, LUK 23:44

The ninth hour: 3 p.m

MAT 27:46 parallel MRK 15:34 See also ACT 3:1, ACT 10:30

Midnight

EXO 11:4 See also PSA 119:62; MAT 25:6; MRK 13:35; ACT 16:25

Other hours of the day

JHN 4:52 the seventh hour: 1 p.m; ; JHN 1:39 the tenth hour: 4 p.m; ; MAT 20:6 the eleventh hour: 5 p.m.

A particular unnamed time of day

MAT 8:13 See also MAT 15:28; LUK 22:14; ACT 16:33; REV 11:13

Hour as an appointed time in God's purpose

The hour of Jesus Christ's suffering

JHN 12:23–12:24 See also MAT 26:45 parallel MRK 14:41; MRK 14:35; JHN 12:27

The unexpected hour of Jesus Christ's return

MAT 24:36 parallel MRK 13:32 See also MAT 24:42–24:44 parallel LUK 12:39–12:40; MAT 25:13

The time before Jesus Christ's return

ROM 13:11 See also 1JN 2:18

The hour of God's judgment

REV 14:7

title: 4951: month
content:
A thirty-day period of the year measured from one new moon to the next. There were twelve months in the year, with a thirteenth inserted periodically to bring the lunar calendar into line with the solar year. Early month names may have been linked with the seasons. After the exile, Babylonian names were adopted.

Months marked by the new moon

NUM 28:11–28:14 Special sacrifices marked the first day of each month. See also PSA 81:3; ISA 66:23; EZK 46:6; AMO 8:5 No work was done on the first day of the month.

Months as periods of 30 days

DEU 21:13 The prescribed period of mourning was 30 days (a month). See also NUM 20:29; DEU 34:8

The first month: Abib/Nisan

EXO 12:2 Abib, meaning “young head of grain” was the Canaanite name for Israel's first month. Modern equivalent: March-April. See also EXO 40:2; NUM 9:1

Feasts in the month of Abib

DEU 16:1 Passover celebrated on 14th Abib. See also EXO 13:4, EXO 23:15 the Feast of Unleavened bread (15th-21st); LEV 23:5–23:6 parallel NUM 28:16–28:17; LEV 23:10–23:11 The first sheaf of barley was brought to the priest on 16th Abib.NEH 2:1; EST 3:7

The second month: Ziv

1KI 6:1 Ziv was the pre-exilic name; the post-exilic name, Iyyar, does not appear in the Bible. Modern equivalent: April-May. See also 1KI 6:37; NUM 9:11 A late Passover might be held on 14th Ziv; 2CH 30:13–30:15 Hezekiah's celebration of the Passover was in the second month.

The third month: Sivan

EST 8:9 Modern equivalent: May-June. See also EXO 19:1; EZK 31:1

Pentecost celebrated in the month of Sivan

LEV 23:15–23:16 on 6th Sivan

The fourth month: Tammuz

EZK 1:1 modern equivalent: June-July; ; EZK 8:14 Tammuz was a fertility goddess; her annual death was mourned in the month named after her.

The fifth month

NUM 33:38 The name of the fifth month, Ab, does not appear in the Bible. Modern equivalent: July-August; ; 2KI 25:8

The sixth month: Elul

NEH 6:15 Modern equivalent: August-September. See also EZK 8:1; HAG 1:1; LUK 1:26

The seventh month: Ethanim

1KI 8:2 Ethanim is the pre-exilic name; its Babylonian equivalent, Tishri, does not appear in the Bible. Modern equivalent: September-October.

Feasts and sacred days in the month of Ethanim

See also LEV 23:23–23:24 parallel NUM 29:1 The Feast of Trumpets (1st). This is celebrated today as the Jewish New Year (Rosh Hashanah); LEV 23:27 parallel NUM 29:7 the Day of Atonement (10th); LEV 23:34 parallel NUM 29:12 the Feast of Tabernacles (15th-21st); LEV 23:36 Solemn Assembly (22nd)

The eighth month: Bul

1KI 6:38 Bul's post-exilic equivalent, Marcheshvan, is not mentioned in the Bible. Modern equivalent: October-November. See also 1KI 12:32–12:33 Jereboam offset Israel's religious calendar by one month. This is the alternative Feast of Tabernacles (15th); ZEC 1:1

The ninth month: Kislev

ZEC 7:1 Modern equivalent: November-December. See also NEH 1:1; HAG 2:18

Hanukkah celebrated in the month of Kislev

JHN 10:22 the Feast of Dedication of the Temple (25th)

The tenth month: Tebeth

EST 2:16 modern equivalent: December-January; ; JER 39:1

The eleventh month: Shebat

DEU 1:3 modern equivalent: January-February; ; ZEC 1:7

The twelfth month: Adar

EST 8:12 Modern equivalent: February-March. See also EST 3:13, EST 9:1

Purim celebrated in the month of Adar

EST 9:20–9:22 the Feast of Purim (14th-15th)

title: 4954: morning
content:
The period of time roughly between dawn and noon. Morning was an assigned time for worship, sacrifice and prayer. The morning light uncovers events of the night and provides opportunity for renewed daily activity. God's blessings are received new every morning, and the start of a new day symbolises renewal and hope.

Morning as the start of a new day

The period of time created by God

GEN 1:5 See also GEN 1:8; EXO 18:13–18:14 The working day is reckoned from morning till evening; EXO 27:21 The night is reckoned from evening till morning; JOB 38:12

The transience of morning mist

ISA 44:22; ; HOS 6:4, HOS 13:3

Morning as a symbol of renewed hope

PSA 30:5 See also PSA 130:6; ISA 21:11–21:12, ISA 26:19

The Messiah brings a new morning

MAL 4:2; 2PE 1:19 See also LUK 1:78–1:79; ROM 13:12; 1JN 2:8; REV 2:28, REV 22:16

The sky in the morning

MAT 16:3

Morning as an opportunity for activity

A time for travel

GEN 19:2, GEN 22:3, GEN 31:55; ; NUM 9:21, NUM 22:21; ; JOS 3:1; ; 1SA 17:20

A time for battle

JOS 8:10–8:14; ; JDG 9:33; ; 2KI 3:21–3:23; ; 2CH 20:20

A time to begin daily labour

RUT 2:7 See also ECC 11:6; MAT 20:1

A time to begin important activity

EXO 9:13, EXO 34:2–34:4 See also EXO 7:15, EXO 8:20; 1KI 18:26; ACT 23:12, ACT 28:23

A time to respond to nightly revelation

GEN 20:8, GEN 28:18, GEN 41:8

A time to administer justice

JER 21:12; ; ZEP 3:5

Morning as a time of revelation

A time of discovery

GEN 29:25; 2KI 19:35 parallel ISA 37:36 See also GEN 19:27–19:28; JDG 6:28; 1SA 5:4; ISA 17:14; 1KI 3:21; 2KI 6:15; ACT 12:18; 1CO 4:5

Morning brings God's revelation

EXO 16:7–16:8 See also NUM 16:5; JOS 7:14; 1SA 9:19; ISA 50:4; EZK 12:8

Morning devotions

A time for offering sacrifices

EZK 46:13–46:15 See also EXO 29:38–29:40, EXO 36:3; NUM 28:3–28:5; 1CH 16:40; 2CH 2:4, 2CH 13:11, 2CH 29:20–29:21; JOB 1:5

A time for prayer

PSA 88:13 See also PSA 5:3, PSA 55:17; MRK 1:35

A time to praise God

PSA 92:1–92:3 See also 1CH 23:30; PSA 59:16, PSA 65:8

Priestly duties each morning

EXO 30:7 burning incense; ; LEV 6:12 making the burnt offering

God's blessings are received new each morning

LAM 3:22–3:23 See also EXO 16:12–16:15 God's provision of manna in the desert; 1KI 17:6 God supplies Elijah with food; PSA 90:14; ISA 33:2

title: 4957: night
content:
The period of darkness between evening and morning. It is generally a time for the cessation of daily activity and for sleep, but, because darkness also gives an opportunity for evil, there is need to be watchful. The term may also be used figuratively to refer to evil or to a period of distress. Night-time also provides opportunity for contemplation and for prayer.

Night distinguished from day

GEN 1:5 See also GEN 1:14–1:18; PSA 74:16

Night as a time for rest and sleep

Rest from daily activity

JDG 19:9; ; ECC 2:23; ; JHN 9:4, JHN 11:9–11:10

Sleep

GEN 15:12, GEN 28:11; ; JOB 4:13

Activity day and night implies continuity and commitment

Weeping

PSA 42:3; ; JER 9:1, JER 14:17

Prayer

NEH 1:6; ; PSA 88:1; ; LUK 18:7; ; 1TH 3:10

Meditation

JOS 1:8; ; PSA 1:2

Worship

LUK 2:37; ; ACT 26:7; ; REV 4:8, REV 7:15

Work

1CH 9:33; ; 1TH 2:9; ; 2TH 3:8

The watches of the night

In OT times: three watches

LAM 2:19 the first watch: sunset to midnight; ; JDG 7:19 the second watch: midnight to 3 a.m; ; EXO 14:24 the last watch: 3 a.m. to dawn

In NT times: four watches

MRK 13:35 See also MAT 14:25 parallel MRK 6:48; LUK 12:38

Night associated with evil

The period when evil is at work

JHN 13:30 Night symbolises the darkness in Judas' soul, and denotes, too, the time of activity of the powers of darkness. See also JOB 30:26; PRO 4:19; LUK 22:53

Opportunity for evil

1CO 4:5 Evil flourishes under cover of darkness. See also PRO 2:13; JHN 3:19–3:20; JOB 24:14 Murder; PRO 7:8–7:9 Adultery; 1TH 5:7 Drunkenness; JER 49:9 parallel OBA 1:5 Theft

Believers do not belong to the night

1TH 5:5 See also JHN 12:35–12:36; ROM 13:12; EPH 5:8; COL 1:13

Believers are to remain watchful

1TH 5:6 See also MAT 24:42–24:44, MAT 25:13; MRK 13:36–13:37

Night associated with distress

Gloom caused by distress

PSA 107:13–107:14; ; ISA 9:2, ISA 59:9

Night as a time of weeping

PSA 6:6, PSA 30:5; ; LAM 1:2

Night as an opportunity to seek God

In prayer

LUK 6:12 See also 1SA 15:11; PSA 22:2, PSA 119:62

In worship

ACT 16:25 See also JOB 35:10; PSA 42:8, PSA 77:6, PSA 139:1–139:2, PSA 149:5; ISA 30:29

In contemplation

PSA 63:6 See also PSA 119:55; ISA 26:9

Night as an opportunity to receive revelation

God's voice at night

1SA 3:4

God's revelation through visions

2CH 7:12; ; DAN 2:19, DAN 7:2; ; ZEC 1:8; ; ACT 18:9, ACT 23:11

God's revelation through dreams

GEN 20:3, GEN 31:24; ; 1KI 3:5; ; JOB 33:15

No night in heaven

REV 21:25, REV 22:5

title: 4960: noon
content:
The mid-point between sunrise and sunset when the sun is at its brightest. Noon is a time to seek shelter from the heat of the day. The darkening of the midday sun and the surprise attack of the enemy at noon are signs of God's judgment. The brilliance of the noonday sun is likened to the glory of Jesus Christ and to the vindication of God's people.

Noon as a time of severe heat

Shelter from the noonday heat

ISA 4:5–4:6 See also ISA 16:3 Moab likens asylum sought from Judah to shelter from the noonday sun; ISA 25:4

Time to rest from the sun's heat

2SA 4:5 See also GEN 18:1SNG 1:7 See also RUT 2:7 Ruth rests from her gleaning at noon; 1SA 11:11 Saul's army halts its destruction of the Ammonites at midday; 1KI 20:16 Israel's enemies relax at midday, unaware of the imminence of battle; MAT 20:12 Workers in the vineyard might have been expected to rest during the hottest part of the day; JHN 4:6 Jesus Christ rests from his journey by the well of Sychar.

Noon as a natural break in the day

1KI 18:26–18:29; ; NEH 8:3

Noon as a time for prayer

PSA 55:16–55:17 The devout Jew prayed three times daily. See also DAN 6:10; ACT 10:9

Noon as a time of God's judgment

A time of unexpected destruction

JER 15:8 Armies usually attacked at dawn; a noon raid would take the people by surprise. See also JER 6:4, JER 20:16; ZEP 2:4

Darkness at noon is a sign of God's judgment

JOB 5:14; AMO 8:9 See also DEU 28:29; ISA 59:9–59:10MAT 27:45 parallels MRK 15:33, LUK 23:44–23:45 The noonday sun is darkened following the crucifixion.

The noonday sun as a symbol of glory

The glory of Jesus Christ

REV 1:16 See also MAT 17:2

The vindication of God's people

JDG 5:31 See also JOB 11:17; PSA 37:6; ISA 58:10; MAT 13:43

Events that occurred at noon

Joseph invites his brothers to eat with him at noon: GEN 43:16, GEN 43:26; JOS 10:12–10:13 The sun stops in the middle of the sky, allowing Joshua to defeat the Amorites; ; 2KI 4:18–4:20 The Shunammite woman's son dies at noon.; ACT 22:6, ACT 26:13

title: 4963: past, the
content:
Jesus Christ transforms the past and sets people free to live in a new way. God's people should remember former blessings but not return to former sins.

Recalling God's work in the past

Remembering God's work of creation

EXO 20:11; ; PSA 104:5–104:9, PSA 136:1–136:9

Remembering God's saving works

PSA 105:5 parallel 1CH 16:12 See also DEU 8:2; JOS 24:2; 1SA 12:6; NEH 9:7; PSA 106:6, PSA 114:1, PSA 136:10, PSA 143:5; ISA 51:1–51:2; MIC 6:5

Remembering God's work in Jesus Christ

2TI 2:8 See also 1CO 11:23–11:26 One of the purposes of the Lord's Supper is to remember Jesus Christ and his death on the cross.

Learning from the past

ROM 15:4 See also DEU 4:9, DEU 32:7; ZEC 1:4; 1CO 10:6, 1CO 10:11; HEB 12:16; 1PE 3:5–3:6; 1JN 3:12

Rediscovering God's word from the past

2KI 22:8–22:13 parallel 2CH 34:15–34:21 See also 2KI 23:1–23:3 parallel 2CH 34:29–34:31; 2KI 23:21–23:23

Leaving the past behind

Jesus Christ sets believers free from the past

2CO 5:17 See also ISA 65:16; GAL 4:3–4:5; COL 1:13–1:14

Warnings not to rely on or be preoccupied with the past

ISA 43:18 This can mean either that God is not to be restricted to his past actions or that Israel should forget their former sins. See also PHP 3:13–3:14

Dangers of the past

The danger of ignoring the past

PSA 78:11 See also EXO 1:8; DEU 32:18; PSA 106:13, PSA 106:21; HOS 2:13

The danger of lapsing into past sins

GAL 4:8–4:9 See also HEB 6:4–6:6; 2PE 1:9; REV 2:4–2:5, REV 3:3

Jesus Christ supersedes God's revelation in the past

Jesus Christ supersedes earlier expectations

ACT 17:30 See also ACT 14:15–14:16

Jesus Christ supersedes earlier covenants

HEB 7:22 See also HEB 1:1–1:2, HEB 8:13, HEB 9:14–9:15, HEB 10:15–10:18; JER 31:33–31:34; HEB 12:24

Jesus Christ reveals God's past mystery

COL 1:26–1:27 See also ROM 16:25–16:26; 1CO 2:7; EPH 1:9, EPH 3:2–3:6, EPH 3:9

title: 4966: present, the
content:
God's blessings are available now and are to be received in faith and contentment. It is dangerous to live only for current blessings and pleasures.

Blessings promised for the present

The blessing of God's presence now

HAG 1:13 See also DEU 31:6; 1KI 8:57

The blessing of Jesus Christ's presence now

MAT 1:23 See also ISA 7:14, ISA 8:8, ISA 8:10; MAT 18:20, MAT 28:20; JHN 14:18; ACT 18:10

The blessing of the Spirit's presence now

JHN 14:16–14:17 See also JHN 14:25–14:27, JHN 16:7, JHN 16:13–16:16

Other blessings given in the present

MRK 10:29–10:30 parallels MAT 19:29, LUK 18:29–18:30 See also EXO 23:25; 1KI 3:13 parallel 2CH 1:12; PSA 121:3–121:8; MAT 6:33; 1TI 4:8; 1PE 1:9

Future blessings are partially fulfilled in the present

EPH 1:13–1:14 See also ROM 8:23, ROM 8:30; 2CO 1:22, 2CO 5:5; EPH 1:3; HEB 12:22–12:24

Prayer for present provision and blessing

LUK 11:3 parallel MAT 6:11 GEN 28:20; ; PRO 30:8; MAT 6:13 parallel LUK 11:4 prayers for spiritual provision

The secret of contentment in the present

PHP 4:10–4:13 See also 1TI 6:6–6:8

The dangers of living for the present

ECC 3:12–3:13 Ecclesiastes in particular reflects upon the emptiness of life without God. Though the good things of the present life are a gift from God and to be valued as such, they cannot bring ultimate fulfilment; LUK 12:15–12:20 See also ECC 2:24, ECC 5:18, ECC 8:15, ECC 9:7, ECC 9:9; ISA 22:13; 1CO 15:32; PHP 3:19; 1TI 6:9–6:10; JAS 4:13–4:17; 1TI 6:17

The challenge of the present

The challenge to respond to God now

2CO 6:2 See also DEU 4:39–4:40, DEU 5:1, DEU 7:11, DEU 10:13, DEU 30:15–30:16; PSA 95:7–95:8; ISA 55:6; HOS 10:12; HEB 3:7

The challenge to serve God now

EPH 5:15–5:16 See also GAL 6:10; COL 4:5

resource.label: 3296: Holy Spirit, work in the world

title: 4969: seasons
content: The main periods of the year which each have their own distinctive weather. In life, the varied periods of human experience.

title: 4970: seasons, of the year
content:
Distinctive periods of the year marked by certain climatic conditions. Seasons are examples of God's perfect order in the world. They represent the balanced cycle of creation which is sustained by God and through which his provision and mercy are made known.

Seasons are appointed by God

Seasons are created by God

PSA 74:16–74:17 See also GEN 1:14, GEN 8:22; PSA 104:19

Seasons are sustained by God

JER 5:24 See also JOB 38:32–38:33; EZK 34:26; ACT 14:17

The agricultural seasons of Palestine

The dry and rainy seasons

JOL 2:23 The rainy season, accounting for over 90 per cent of the national rainfall, runs from October to March/April. Its beginning is marked by the autumn rains in October/November; the lighter spring showers in April/May announce the coming of the dry season. See also EZR 10:13; HOS 6:3; JAS 5:7LEV 26:3–26:4; DEU 11:13–11:14, DEU 28:12

Seedtime and harvest

PRO 20:4 Ploughing and sowing take place from October to December when the autumn rains have begun to soften the ground. Most crops are harvested between April and June. See also EXO 34:21; NUM 13:20; JOB 5:26; PSA 1:3

Spring

Spring rain as a sign of blessing

JOB 29:23 The gentle spring rains signal the approach of harvest; ; PRO 16:15; ; JER 3:3; ; ZEC 10:1

Spring as a time of growth

SNG 2:11–2:13 Spring marks a pleasant interval between the heavy winter rains and the onset of the dry season. See also PRO 27:25; JOL 2:22; MAT 24:32 parallels MRK 13:28, LUK 21:29–21:30

Spring as a time for war

2SA 11:1 parallel 1CH 20:1 The time following the spring harvest (May/June), when agricultural commitments are reduced; ; 1KI 20:26; ; 2KI 13:20

Festivals celebrated in spring

LEV 23:5–23:6 parallel NUM 28:16–28:17 Passover and Unleavened Bread; ; LEV 23:10 offering the firstfruits of the barley harvest; ; LEV 23:15–23:16 parallel DEU 16:9–16:10 The Feast of Weeks celebrated the ingathering of the grain harvest.

Summer

A period of intense heat

PSA 32:4 See also JOB 6:17

A time for harvesting summer fruit

JER 8:20 See also PRO 10:5; JER 40:10–40:12 Grapes, olives and figs are harvested in July/September; MIC 7:1

A time to store harvested grain

PRO 6:8, PRO 30:25

Autumn

The breaking of the summer drought

PSA 84:6 Autumn is the start of the rainy season and corresponds to the time of ploughing and sowing.

Festivals celebrated in autumn

See also LEV 23:24 parallel NUM 29:1 the Feast of Trumpets; LEV 23:27 parallel NUM 29:7 the Day of Atonement; LEV 23:34 parallel NUM 29:12 the Feast of Tabernacles

Winter

A time of inclement weather

JOB 37:6–37:10 See also ISA 55:10; JER 36:22

A time when travelling is difficult

MAT 24:20 parallel MRK 13:18; ; 2TI 4:21; ; ACT 27:12; ; 1CO 16:6; ; TIT 3:12

Celebration of the Feast of Dedication

JHN 10:22 The Feast of Dedication (Hannukah) commemorates the rededication of the temple by Judas Maccabeus in 165 B.C.

title: 4971: seasons, of life
content:
Appropriate or appointed periods of time which are part of the variety and development of human life and experience and which influence human affairs. The times and seasons for individuals and nations are set by God, who works all things together towards the final fulfilment of his purposes.

The seasons of life are appointed by God

The seasons of individual lives

ECC 3:1–3:8; PSA 31:15 See also JOB 14:5; PSA 139:16; ECC 3:11; ROM 8:28; JAS 4:13–4:15

The seasons of nations and the world

DAN 2:21; ACT 17:26 See also DAN 4:17, DAN 7:25; ACT 1:7

A time to die

The transience of human life

PSA 103:15–103:16 See also JOB 7:6–7:8, JOB 14:1–14:2; PSA 37:2, PSA 102:11; ECC 6:12

Death comes at its appointed time

JOB 5:26 See also GEN 47:29; 1KI 2:1; LUK 12:20

Times of sadness and joy

A time to mourn

GEN 27:41, GEN 37:34, GEN 50:3–50:4; ; DEU 34:8; ; JER 4:28

A time to rejoice

JHN 16:21–16:22 See also DEU 28:47; EST 9:22; ISA 61:2–61:3

A time to speak

PRO 15:23, PRO 25:11; ; 2TI 4:2 Christians are to be always ready to share the gospel.

Times appointed for the world

The rise and fall of nations

DAN 2:37–2:45, DAN 8:19–8:25, DAN 11:2–11:4, DAN 11:24–11:27; ; LUK 21:24

The time to receive God's salvation

2CO 6:2 See also ISA 49:8, ISA 55:6; HEB 4:7

The time of God's judgment

DEU 32:35; ; EZK 30:3; ; ZEP 2:2; ; ACT 17:31

Times appointed for God's people

Times for worship

LEV 23:4 See also EXO 34:18; NUM 9:2–9:3, NUM 10:10

Times of blessing

PSA 102:13 See also EZK 34:26; ACT 3:19, ACT 7:17

A time of vindication

DAN 7:21–7:22 See also ACT 1:6; ROM 8:18; 1PE 5:6

Times of testing

PSA 66:10; ; EZK 21:3; ; MAT 13:21 parallels MRK 4:17, LUK 8:13; ; 1PE 1:6–1:7; ; REV 2:10

Times for withdrawal or solitude

MRK 1:35 See also 1KI 17:2–17:6; MRK 6:30–6:32; GAL 1:17

Waiting for God's appointed time

HAB 2:3 See also ROM 8:23–8:25; HEB 9:28, HEB 11:13

Times appointed for Jesus Christ

The appointed time of his birth

GAL 4:4

The appointed time of his death

JHN 12:23 See also MAT 26:18; MRK 14:41; LUK 9:51; JHN 2:4, JHN 7:6–7:8, JHN 12:27, JHN 13:1

The appointed time of his return

LUK 21:8; ; ACT 3:20–3:21, ACT 17:31; ; 1CO 4:5; ; REV 1:3

title: 4975: week
content:
The pattern of weeks provides a useful framework for marking time in agricultural and religious life. The story of creation is described in terms of seven days and illustrates a cycle of work and rest. Feasts and other rituals last for a certain number of weeks.

The seven days of creation

GEN 2:1–2:3 See also GEN 1:5, GEN 1:8, GEN 1:13, GEN 1:19, GEN 1:23, GEN 1:31

The Sabbath-rest

DEU 5:12–5:14 parallel EXO 20:8–20:11 See also EXO 34:21, EXO 35:2; HEB 4:3–4:4, HEB 4:8–4:10

Seven-day intervals or periods

After building the ark Noah had seven days' notice of the flood

GEN 7:4

Noah sent out the dove at seven-day intervals

See also GEN 8:8–8:12

Marching round Jericho

HEB 11:30 See also JOS 6:12–6:15

Other examples of seven-day intervals or periods

EXO 7:25, EXO 29:30; ; 1SA 10:8, 1SA 11:3, 1SA 13:8; ; 1KI 20:29; ; 1CH 9:25; ; ACT 20:6, ACT 21:4, ACT 28:14

Rituals lasting seven days

Menstrual impurity

LEV 15:19

Initial period of uncleanness after childbirth

LEV 12:1–12:2 This would be fourteen days if the child were a girl (Lev 12:5).

Mourning

GEN 50:10 See also 1SA 31:13 parallel 1CH 10:12; JOB 2:13; EZK 3:15

The Feast of Weeks

DEU 16:9–16:10 See also EXO 34:22; LEV 23:15–23:21; NUM 28:26–28:31

Feasts lasting seven days

Wedding feasts

GEN 29:27 See also JDG 14:10–14:12

The Feast of Unleavened Bread

EXO 34:18 See also LEV 23:5–23:8; 2CH 30:21–30:23, 2CH 35:17; EZR 6:22

The Feast of Tabernacles

LEV 23:34 See also LEV 23:36; NEH 8:18

Dedication of the temple

1KI 8:65 parallel 2CH 7:8–7:9

Daniel's ``weeks''

DAN 9:24 “sevens” may also be translated “weeks”. See also DAN 9:25–9:27

God promises that times and seasons will always continue

JER 5:24 See also GEN 8:22

title: 4978: year
content:
A period of twelve months, with additional periods inserted to bring the lunar year into line with the solar year. Certain calendar years have special significance. The term also denotes an indeterminate time when God will intervene to restore his people and punish his enemies.

The year made up of 12 months

1KI 4:7 See also 1CH 27:1–27:15; EST 2:12; DAN 4:29; REV 22:2

The start of the year

The start of the religious year

EXO 12:2 The exodus from Egypt in Abib (March April) marked a new beginning in the life of God's people. See also LEV 23:5 parallel NUM 28:16 Abib is referred to as the first month; 2CH 35:1; EZR 6:19

The start of the agricultural year

EXO 23:16 The Feast of Ingathering (or Tabernacles) was celebrated in the seventh month. See also EXO 34:22; LEV 23:23–23:24 parallel NUM 29:1

God's blessing through the year

PSA 65:9–65:13 See also DEU 11:11–11:15, DEU 28:12

A thousand years as one day

PSA 90:4; ; 2PE 3:8

Special years

The sabbatical year

LEV 25:1–25:7 See also EXO 23:10–23:11; LEV 25:20–25:22 God's provision for the seventh yearDEU 15:1DEU 15:9; NEH 10:31; DEU 31:10–31:13 the law of God to be read every seventh year at the Feast of Tabernacles

The Year of Jubilee

LEV 25:8–25:10 The Year of Jubilee began in the seventh month. See also LEV 25:11–25:12 The Year of Jubilee is a sabbath year; LEV 25:13 Each Hebrew is to return to his family property; LEV 25:28 Land is returned to its original owner; LEV 25:40 Hebrew slaves are to be released; LEV 25:54

Hebrew slaves released in the seventh year

EXO 21:2 parallel DEU 15:12 See also JER 34:14

The year of the LORD's favour

ISA 61:1–61:3 This has echoes of the Year of Jubilee and points to the time when God will restore his people. See also LUK 4:18–4:21; ISA 49:8–49:9; 2CO 6:2ISA 63:4 Salvation for God's people is accompanied by judgment on his enemies.

The year of punishment

See also JER 11:23, JER 23:12, JER 48:44
